summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques the statistical justification for New York's Stop and Frisk policy, arguing that Bloomberg and Kelly use a flawed evaluation metric by crediting the policy for declining murder rates without establishing causation. She points out that murder rates depend on many factors including crack epidemics and improved medical care, and that recent decreases in Stop and Frisk incidents haven't caused murder spikes. O'Neil proposes an alternative metric—trust in police—which reveals the policy's devastating impact on minority communities. She highlights the striking statistic that NYPD stopped more young black men than actually exist in the city's population, creating an expectation of being stopped at least once per year for black New Yorkers.
key_points: Evaluation metrics are crucial to any model, and choosing the wrong one leads to optimizing for the wrong outcomes
Attributing New York's declining murder rate to Stop and Frisk is a tenuous causal argument unsupported by evidence
When Stop and Frisk decreased drastically, murder rates did not increase, undermining the claimed causal link
An alternative evaluation metric—trust in police—reveals the policy's discriminatory impact on minority communities
The number of stops of young black men in 2011 exceeded the entire city population of young black men, meaning statistical expectation of being stopped at least once annually
money_quotes: They use a sleight of hand with respect to the evaluation metric of the policy.
A poor choice for it can have dire consequences – you could end up optimizing to the wrong thing.
Ascribing credit for the plunging death rate to Stop and Frisk is a tenuous causal argument.
Since Stop and Frisk events have decreased drastically recently, we haven't seen the murder rate shoot up.
400,000 innocent black and Latino New Yorkers were stopped last year under this policy.
The number of stops of young black men exceeded the entire city population of young black men (168,126 as compared to 158,406).
If I'm a black guy I have an expectation of getting stopped and frisked at least once per year. How does that make me trust cops?
Let's choose an evaluation method closer to what we can actually control, and let's optimize to it.
key_insight: Policies justified by data can be deeply flawed when authorities choose evaluation metrics that obscure harm and falsely claim credit for outcomes they didn't cause.
